author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
Now you got me wondering how many amps a Power Supply would have to put out to do this.

Because I seen batteries kersplodey, too. My guess is, 30-75 amps should do well. And I'd go with a Constant Current PSU.
But this looks like it might be a Poor Man's Answer for sheet metal work.

author: @benniedonald
text: If you have welded enough you know about how much your eyes can take before getting burned. Of course most know you can actually see the ark through your eyelids when closed. So tacking very short tacks can be done safely. However if you have never welded and gotten your retinas burned. Don't try this at home. Because the pain is excruciating and hard to soothe even with potatoes.

author: @petersack5074
text: good. now, grab another carbon rod, and, grind down one end of each into a point. secure one pointed up, and place the pieces to be welded on it. Use the other pointed end, as the 'stinger' and then weld them together.....should work much better.....would make a good 'spot welder' google around, there is more than a few...

author: @downrangeops
text: When I was a young apprentice auto electrician in Africa, we used to cut open D cell batteries and take out the carbon rod. We also needed two pieces of wire and two crocodile clips. We would sharpen the tip of the carbon rod on a grindstone. This then made an excellent portable pocket sized soldering iron in the field. This was before gas soldering irons appeared. Many times, working in sugarcane fields at all hours, against tight deadlines, this little device saved the day for me and the farmers. White man's magic!

text: Hi Robert, keep up the good work. You are a pretty smart man perhaps you might have some thoughts on a project I am working on. I am trying to build a driver circuit  200v to 400v 80KHz. I can get an inverter in the voltage range but with a maximum 20KHz frequency or VFD to 150KHz but only 30V. One option is to run both and use a field effect transistor to manage the inverter output, but for the life of me I can't find any info about how this configuration might effect the final Hertze frequency. Any thoughts you might have would be greatly appreciated. Cheers John

author: @ralphwaters8905
text: I've used 2 car batteries in series (24 VDC) for stick welding in the field, and it works VERY well.  Output seems to run about 120 Amps, which is about right for your average E6013 rod.  If you have jumper cables with clamps having a hole in each jaw, you can put the end of your electrode in the hole and the other jaw will clamp it tightly at an angle.  Easy Peezy!

replies.author: @cuzz63
replies.text: I have done the same thing. I got the idea from using a fork truck battery to weld at work which was 24 volts. The higher voltage makes a better arc.

author: @johnwilson3668
text:
I have a carbon arc welder which I bought in the 1970's. Both positive and negative connect to the welding torch . The carbon rods are at a 30 deg angle to each other. When you press                        the grip the carbon rods move together producing a long flame. I used it in the past for brazing and heating larger pieces of metal for heat treating .Marvellous technology and just connected
 to a 12v battery

author: @nlo114
text: In 1972 I was apprenticed to Post office telephones. On one remote exchange construction job the battery cells had to be stood in their box ready for the power-man to commission. He had a carbon rod 'torch', with a few feet of stout cable and a battery clip. He'd drop the lead links on one by one, then use 3 cells to produce the arc, move the clip, add another link etc.

author: @EyesOnReality
text: Fantastic! Yet another Real-World example, proving Negative Goes To Positive (as also shown in electrolysis). Yet universities are still teaching positive going to negative (right-hand-rule that should be the left-hand-rule) as, for example,  capacitors (C=KC) flows Anode to Cathode. In electronics its no matter as long as diodes are correctly polarized for flow, yet in physics (as in the magnetic B-Field direction), it does matter. Its only inside a battery is when the right-hand-rule accurately applies. Thanks for another great video Robert.

replies.author: @abyssmanur3965
replies.text:
A proper carbon arc rod is  about 300mm long and can last quite a few hours depending on what amps your running through it. It doesnt deposit material but just heats like a gas torch.
The old carbon arc lamps operated in a near vacuum and could last much longer.

author: @jasonwitt8619
text: WOW, so now you got me wondering can I use my cheap little car battery charger to do this, hmmm

replies.author: @Jakob6174
replies.text: I highly doubt it, car batteries typically supply 300-700 amps for a cold start but can output over 1500 amps with a dead short (Pretty much this situation). That is ≈20kW of power. If your charger is plugged into the mains, you are limited to about ⅒ of that power.

replies.author: @fidelcatsro6948
replies.text: it might damage the battery too...dead shorting

replies.author: @wendellhammond7853
replies.text: Most 12 volt battery chargers only puts out 14.4 to 15.2 volts at 10 amps not a lot of power not enough to weld with what will probably happen is you will get a new charger for x-mass

replies.author: @petersack5074
replies.text: yes. Place that large booster charger box,(200 amp start-boost) on 12 volt BOOST,  then try this...should work good, if your charger is ok. get 2 of these carbon rods, sharpen one end of each on grinder/angle grinder etc.   make a mount, and use both sharpened points, and 'happy welding '........

author: @andrewbeaton3302

author: @davidschwartz5127
text: Back in the day when we were kids, we would use 6-volt car batteries and coat hanger wire for electrodes to weld on our hot rods repairs until one day when I was welding up a rust hole in the floor pan. For some reason, I blew the battery to smithereens. Luckily no one was harmed in the explosion but there was one big mess in my father's garage. Dad turned it into a learning moment by explaining that shorting out a lead-acid battery like I was doing causes hydrogen gas to be produced in excessive amounts if sparks from a loose wire connection twisted around the battery post apparently set off an explosion inside the battery... And never, ever do that again! That Christmas, Dad gifted me and my brother a 150 amp Miller buzz box welder,  it's over 55 years old now and still works well.

replies.author: @stewartcaldwell5299
replies.text: Perhaps longer wires, and battery outdoors? (Sized like jumper cables).

replies.author: @FogHat1980

author: @NinjaNige
text:
Great Video As Always Rob.. Just One Thought Here.. Shorting Out The Battery Like That Could Cause Damage And If Overused May Cause An Explosive Ending.
If You Use A Capacitor Bank Then The Risk Of A Explosion Or Major Battery Damage Would Decrease And Also Better Controlled Wealds..
But An Awsome Idea For A Very Quick Tack Job Just To Get You Out Of Trouble.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
The theory works but there are issues.  
1 you want to avoid heat damage to the battery so the heat made should be to a small area and only enough to weld that small spot. 

The theory behind spot welding is maximizing current through a small spot for a short duration. Max current means minimizing  resistance in the weld cable and the spot welder tips.  As for the tips Often what's used is copper tips analogous to a copper nail.  They pass a lot of current through to a small spot on the work piece, with the work piece having a  lower conductivity means and so much current through a small spot means the energy gets dumped into the work piece at that spot.  Since your thinking about spot welding nickle to a battery top you really need two copper nail like electrods whose tips get fairly close to eachother touching the work piece briefly at the same time.

replies.author: @spex357
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 I have some copper nails thanks for the suggestion.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@spex357  👍 Clip the head of the nail off and hope that the nail's crossection is thick enough to carry the current and minimize heating in the nail.  

Another thing with thick copper ground to a somewhat pointed tip, any heat generated in the tip quickly gets wicked away.

author: @3DLasers

Why are you welding it from the Negative Electrode ?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: because I don't really know what I am doing - I'm not a welder - but I have been told thin bit from negative - thick bits from positive - but I have also been told only positive and I have been told only negative - so who knows - I just guessed lol

replies.author: @77pete7
replies.text: From my very limited understanding, Rob has heard correctly. This is the way for thin metal. Doing it the other way causes more heat build up in, and often melts a whole right through the work. With the negative welding, the heat build up is in the electrode instead, thus protecting the metal.

text: Great idea for spot welding. The gloves are great PPE but wear some glasses or a welders auto darkening mask. Watch the UV too. Carbon rod UV medical lights where prescribed as a cure for all sorts of medical conditions in the sixties until banned because UV burns.

author: @snoddyification
text:
As always THANKS.  
Seems close to fusion welding technique?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: is that right? cheers mate

replies.author: @snoddyification
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering in fusion stud welding, (attatch studs to steel to bind onsite poured concrete to steel support structures, eg bridge decks to girders), an extremely high amperage is applied to the stud, wich is in contact with the gun.   
Earth to structure.
No shield gas, no flux powder, just a tiny bally of ally alloy at the stud contact point.
Place stud in location and sueeze trigger.
Full fusion weld.
Bash stud over flat with a sledge hammer, won't detatch.
The similarity for me is the lack of flux.
